items.caption:
Most athletes overcomplicate their first email. 

Most coaches want the opposite. 

They want enough information to quickly determine whether your athlete fits what they're recruiting. 

Keep it simple.
Make it easy to evaluate.
Make it easy to respond. 

Because recruiting isn't about sending the perfect email. 

It's about creating opportunities and building relationships with coaches. 

That's just one of the many topics we cover inside the College Recruiting Playbook. 

Comment PLAN and we'll send you information about the College Recruiting Playbook built specifically for your athlete's sport and graduation year. 

#CollegeRecruiting #StudentAthlete #SportsParents #RecruitingTips #PremierAthletes

Most families hear “D3” and immediately think “lesser opportunity.”

That is a mistake.

Some of the best college fits in the country are sitting at the Division 3 level.

Strong academics.
Competitive athletics.
Great campuses.
Real player development.
And a college experience that actually makes sense for the athlete.

The problem is too many families are chasing the logo instead of chasing the right opportunity.

D1 is not the goal.

The goal is finding a school where your athlete can play, develop, graduate, and actually enjoy the next four years.

Here are 5 underrated D3 schools more high school athletes should know:

5. University of Redlands — California

4) Berry College — Georgia

3. Endicott College — Massachusetts

2) Salisbury University — Maryland

1. Trinity University — Texas

Do not overlook a school just because it does not have the division label you thought you wanted.

The right fit beats the wrong level every single time.

If you want guidance on how to start gaining traction with schools like this, comment “PLAY” and I’ll send you more information.
items.transcript: Here are five underrated Division III schools parents and high school athletes should not overlook. Before you say it's Division III, that is exactly where a lot of families get this wrong. You're not choosing a division, you are chasing the right opportunity for your athlete to play, compete, and have a great college experience. Number five, University of Redlands in California. Strong academics, competitive athletics, and a real West Coast campus experience. Number four, Berry College in Georgia. This is a Southeast school more athletes should know about. Good campus, strong student experience, and a place where the right athlete can develop. Number three, Etiquette College in Massachusetts. Etiquette has a lot of momentum, Northeast location, strong athletic program, and a school that takes the student-athlete experience seriously. Number two, Salisbury University in Maryland. Do not confuse D3 with easy. Salisbury is serious athletics. If you can play there, you can really play. And number one, Trinity University in Texas. High academics, strong athletics, great city, and one of the better D3 fits in the South. The point is simple. Stop chasing the logo. Stop chasing the division. Start finding the school where your athlete can actually play, develop, graduate, and have a great college experience. If you want guidance on how to start gaining traction with schools like this, comment play, and I'll send you more information. Good luck.

items.caption:
"Later" sounds harmless. 

Until the school list isn't built.

The coaches haven't been contacted.

The video still isn't finished.

And the opportunities your athlete hoped for are already filling up.

Most families don't miss opportunities because their athlete isn't talented enough.

They miss opportunities because they never built a recruiting plan.

The families who feel the most confident during recruiting usually aren't the ones who know everything.

They're the ones who started early, stayed organized, and followed a system.

The goal isn't just getting recruited.

It's helping your athlete find the right college fit and experience everything college athletics has to offer.

Comment PLAN and we'll send you information about the College Recruiting Playbook built specifically for your athlete's sport and graduation year.

#CollegeRecruiting #StudentAthlete #SportsParents #RecruitingPlan #PremierAthletes

Most families overlook Division 2 because they are too focused on Division 1.

That is a mistake.

There are D2 schools all over the country where student-athletes can get a great education, compete at a high level, develop, and have an incredible college experience.

The key is knowing which schools actually fit your athlete.

Not every school is the right fit.

Not every coach is the right fit.

Not every roster has opportunity.

That is why you need a plan.

If you want to get your athlete on the radar of college coaches within the next 30 days, my College Recruiting Playbook is open for July.

Comment PLAY and I’ll send you more information.
items.transcript: Here are five underrated D2 schools you should not overlook. If you are a parent trying to help your student-athlete play in college, don't miss these schools. Number five, Georgia College. This is a strong option for families who want a smaller feel, strong academics, and a real Division II athletic experience without chasing massive logos. Number four, Michigan Tech. This one is not flashy, but if your athlete is serious about academics, especially STEM, engineering, business, or long-term career ROI, this is a hidden gem. Number three, Westchester in Pennsylvania. Public school value, strong athletics, and a location that gives athletes access to Philadelphia job market after graduation. Number two, Western Washington. This is one of the most overlooked athletic departments in Division II. Strong academics, strong athletics, and a great overall student-athlete experience for the right fit. And number one, Augustana in South Dakota. Augustana is one of the schools that does not always get talked about nationally, but when you look at the academics and you look at the athletic success in the campus community, it checks a lot of the boxes. And here is the bigger lesson. Stop only chasing the schools everybody already knows, D1s, all those. The best recruiting fit is not always the biggest brands. It is the school where your athlete can play, develop, graduate, actually enjoy the college experience. And if you are looking to get on the radar of these college coaches within the next 30 days, you need to follow a proven plan. My college recruiting playbook is open for July. Comment, play down below, and I'll send you more information. Good luck.

items.caption:
Most recruiting success comes from doing simple things consistently.

Building a better school list.
Sending another email.
Updating video.
Improving grades.
Following a plan.

None of it is flashy.

But these are often the things that move the process forward.

Share this with a family that feels behind.

#CollegeRecruiting #RecruitingTips #StudentAthlete #SportsParents #PremierAthletes

The NCAA just changed college eligibility forever… and I’m curious where parents and athletes stand on this. 👀

For years, the rule was simple:

➡️ 5 years to play 4 seasons.

Now, Division I athletes are moving toward a model that gives them 5 years to play 5 seasons, with eligibility tied more closely to age and when their clock starts.

The NCAA says this will eliminate the chaos of extra years, waivers, and 25- or 26-year-old athletes still competing.

Some people think this is long overdue.

Others think it could create an entirely new set of problems for recruiting.

As a former Division I coach, scout, and recruiter, I can see arguments on both sides.

But what do YOU think?

👇 Drop your answers below:

1️⃣ Is this a good change or a bad change?

2️⃣ Should athletes get five full seasons?

3️⃣ Will this help or hurt recruiting opportunities for high school athletes?

4️⃣ Should Division II and Division III adopt this model too?

I want to hear from parents, athletes, coaches, and former players.

The recruiting landscape is changing again.

Let’s talk about it.
items.transcript: Parents, athletes, what do you guys think of the new age-based eligibility model that just came out from NCAA for Division I sports only right now, but I do expect Division II to follow suit and then eventually Division III. We'll see what they do. And there's a lot of opinions out there from coaches and scouts and things like that, but I have a lot of parents and athletes that follow me and I want to hear what you guys think and any questions that you have. And if you have no idea what I'm talking about, a new model just came out for college athletes of basically how long they could play and when their clock starts. The old model was you had five years to complete four years of eligibility. In the new models, you have five years to play five years of college athletics right now at the Division I level, but it also matters when your clock starts. And the reason that this change was because things were getting out of control with kids being 25, 26, 27, and all these different waivers out. And just a real quick synopsis of when basically your clock starts is when you graduate high school, the academic year, the next year after that, the fall, basically when you become a freshman, your clock starts. Or when you turn 19 and the next academic year starts for college, that's when it starts there. But I want to hear what you guys think. Go on and comment below on any questions that you have.

items.caption:
Most recruiting stress doesn't come from recruiting.

It comes from trying to remember everything.

Who replied.

Who didn't.

Which camps matter.

Which schools fit.

What needs to happen next.

The clearer the plan, the quieter the process feels.

#CollegeRecruiting #StudentAthlete #SportsParents #RecruitingTips #PremierAthletes

Your athlete does not need the biggest logo to have a great college experience.

They need the right fit.

A place where they can compete, grow, be supported, graduate, and actually enjoy the next four years.

Too many families get caught chasing the same schools…

SEC. ACC. Big Ten. Big 12.

And I get it. Those schools are visible. They’re exciting. They’re the names everyone knows.

But if your recruiting plan is built only around Power Four logos, you may be missing some incredible Division I opportunities.

There are schools outside the Power Four with:

✅ strong academics
✅ competitive athletics
✅ great campus life
✅ real student-athlete support
✅ opportunities to play, develop, and build a future

These are the schools more families should be studying:

1. Richmond
2. Santa Clara
3. University of Denver
4. William & Mary
5. Villanova

The right school is not always the biggest brand.

It’s the school where your athlete can become the best version of themselves.

Athletically.
Academically.
Socially.
Personally.

Stop chasing logos.

Start studying fit.

Give me a follow if you want more guidance on helping your athlete succeed and have a great college experience.
items.transcript: Here are five underrated D1 schools you should pay attention to. Most families are only looking at the same schools, SEC, ACC, Big 10, Big 12, and I get it. I coach and recruited at one. They're super shiny and sexy. But if you're only chasing power four logos, you're missing a lot of really good D1 opportunities. There are schools outside of the power four that have strong athletics, strong academics, great campus life, and a real student athlete experience. Here are five underrated D1 schools more families should be looking at. Number one is Richmond. High level academics, strong athletics, and a great student athlete graduation rate. This is one of those schools where fit matters more than the name recognition. Number two, Santa Clara. Strong academics, great location, and a really good athletic tradition, especially on the West Coast. Number three, University of Denver. Yes, they are D1. One of the biggest non-football athletic departments in the country. Hockey, lacrosse, soccer, skiing, golf, tennis. They win across multiple sports. Number four, William & Mary. High academic school, strong student support, and a great option for the right athlete who wants serious academics in Division I sports. Number five, Villanova. Most people only think basketball, but Villanova is strong across athletics, academics, and student outcome. The lesson here is simple, all right? The right school is not always the biggest logo. It's the school where your athlete can develop, compete, graduate, and actually enjoy the next four years. Stop chasing names, start studying fit. Good luck.

items.caption:
🏆 NATIONAL CHAMPIONS!

The Oklahoma Sooners are back on top of college baseball.

After an incredible run through Omaha, Oklahoma captured the 2026 College World Series title and proved once again why they’re one of the premier programs in the country.

From elite talent and clutch performances to outstanding coaching and execution under pressure, the Sooners earned every bit of this championship.

Winning a national title isn’t just about talent. It’s about culture, preparation, development, and performing when the lights are brightest.

Congratulations to Coach Skip Johnson, the Oklahoma coaching staff, players, and the entire Sooner community on an unforgettable season.

 #CollegeWorldSeries #OklahomaBaseball #NCAABaseball #NationalChampions #PremierAthletes

Travel/Club is NOT the same thing as being recruited.

And this is where a lot of families get burned.

They spend thousands of dollars chasing tournaments, hotels, flights, showcases, and “exposure”…

But after the weekend is over, they still can’t answer the most important questions:

Who actually watched my son play?

Were they recruiting his position?

Were they recruiting his grad class?

Was he a real fit for those schools?

Did any meaningful conversation happen after?

Because just playing in a tournament does not mean college coaches are evaluating you.

Travel ball can be great for development, competition, and reps.

But it is not a recruiting strategy by itself.

The goal is not to play more games.

The goal is to get in front of the RIGHT schools with a clear plan.

Parents, don’t confuse activity with traction.

If your athlete wants to play in college, you need more than tournaments.

You need a plan.
items.transcript: I was just at a travel tournament and I was talking to a parent. And honestly, I feel bad for the lie a lot of families fall for. That lie is this. You have to play travel to play in college. And that is just not true. Travel can be helpful. It can give your athlete reps, development of the right coaches, competition, and sometimes exposure. But travel ball by itself is not a recruiting plan. There are too many teams. There are too many tournaments. There are too many games for college coaches to be everywhere. So here's the question every parent should ask. How many college coaches actually watch my athlete play this weekend or whatever tournament you're at? Even more important, were they schools that fit them athletically, academically, geographically, financially, all of those things? Because getting seen by random coaches is not the goal. Getting in front of the right schools is the goal. So don't fall into the trap of thinking more tournaments automatically means more recruiting. Play travel if it helps them develop. But don't confuse playing travel with being recruited.

items.caption:
Soccer parents, these numbers should get your attention.

There are hundreds of thousands of high school soccer players trying to earn a college opportunity.

And these are the top 10 states by total high school soccer participation:

1. California — 104,802
2. Texas — 89,284
3. Pennsylvania — 38,535
4. Florida — 37,925
5. New York — 37,655
6. Illinois — 35,517
7. New Jersey — 33,116
8. Ohio — 32,339
9. Massachusetts — 26,404
10. Michigan — 26,021

Now here’s what parents need to understand…

More players means more competition.

College coaches are not just randomly discovering every good player.

They are looking for athletes who fit their level, their roster needs, their position needs, their academic profile, and their recruiting timeline.

That means your athlete needs more than talent.

They need a plan.

A realistic school list.
A strong highlight video.
Personalized coach communication.
Smart event decisions.
Consistent follow-up.

The goal is not just to “get exposure.”

The goal is to get noticed by the right schools.

I help parents guide their athlete to the right college fit and a great college experience.

Follow for more.

Parents of high school athletes…

The “best” college is not always D1 or the biggest logos

It is the school where your athlete can:

Compete
Grow
Get a meaningful degree
Build lifelong relationships
And actually enjoy the college experience

That is why we put together this list of the Top 10 Universities in Ohio based on:

Academics
Athletics
Student satisfaction

Some families only chase the Power 4 names.

But the right fit could be a Big Ten school.

It could be a MAC school.

It could be a private D1.

It could be a high-academic D3.

The key is knowing where your athlete actually fits.

Because recruiting without a plan usually turns into guessing.

And guessing gets expensive.

If your son or daughter wants to play college sports, start asking better questions:

Does this school fit academically?
Does this level fit athletically?
Is the school recruiting my athlete’s position?
Can we afford it?
Would my athlete be happy there without sports?

That is how you build a real college list.

Not by chasing logos.

By finding fit.

A lot of families ask:

“How do we know if a college coach is really interested?”

Here’s the honest answer…

Interest usually does not start with an offer.

It starts with awareness.

A coach has to know who you are first.

Then they have to believe you fit what they are looking for.

That means your position, grad year, grades, size, athleticism, skill set, stats, video, projectability, and level all matter.

This is where a lot of athletes get frustrated.

They are reaching out to schools that may not be recruiting their position.

Or schools where they are not an athletic fit.

Or schools where they are not an academic fit.

Or schools that are simply not realistic right now.

That does not mean the athlete cannot play in college.

It usually means the target list is wrong.

When a coach is truly interested, you will usually see the communication become more personal and more direct:

They respond to your emails.
They ask for video.
They ask for your schedule.
They text you.
They call you.
They invite you to campus.
They want to see you at camp.
They talk specifically about where you fit in their program.

But before all of that happens, you need a plan to get on the right coaches’ radar.

Not just any coach.

The right coach.

At the right school.

At the right level.

For your position.

In your grad class.

That is where recruiting starts to become a strategy instead of just hope.

If your athlete is trying to get more interest from college coaches, start by asking:

Are we targeting schools where he actually fits?

Because if the answer is no, you are probably going to feel like nothing is working.

Comment PLAYBOOK, and I’ll send you a plan to start generating more interest.
items.transcript: So how do you know if a college coach is actually interested in you? First, you have to understand this. Before a coach can be interested in you, they have to know about you. They have to know who you are. That sounds obvious, but a lot of athletes are sitting around wondering why coaches are not reaching out when they have never actually gotten in front of the right schools or they know about the athlete. So the first question is, do college coaches know who you are? And if they don't, what is your plan to change that? Because interest usually starts when a coach sees something they like, your video, your measurable, your physicality, your stats, your projectability, your position, your grades, your body, your athleticism, your ability to fit what they are recruiting. But here's where a lot of families get frustrated. They are chasing schools where the athlete is not actually a fit. If a school is not recruiting your position, if your metrics do not match what they are usually recruiting, if you are not an academic fit, if you are not realistic for that level, then you may not get interest no matter how many emails you send. When a coach is truly interested, you will usually start to see more direct communication. They may start emailing you personally, then texting, then calling. Then they may invite you to campus for a college visit. They may want to see you at a camp. They may talk more specifically about where you fit in their program. And eventually, if the fit is right, they can lead to an offer. But it does not start with the offer. It starts with exposure, them getting to know you. It starts with getting in front of the right schools. And it starts with targeting programs where you actually fit athletically, academically, geographically, and financially. So if you are trying to build recruiting interest, start local. Start with schools that can actually see you in a geographical sense. Start with schools that recruit your position. Start with schools where your ability matches what they are looking for. Because that is the goal. It's not just sending emails to college coaches around the country. The goal is to get in front of the right ones. If you're looking for a proven plan to start generating more interest in offers, comment, playbook, and I'll send it to you.

items.caption:
Generic interest usually creates generic responses.

When an athlete can clearly explain why a school makes sense, the conversation changes.

It shows preparation.

It shows maturity.

It shows the athlete is thinking beyond the logo.

Coaches notice when an email feels specific instead of copied and pasted.

They notice when the athlete understands the program, the academic fit, the roster, the location, and why that school could actually make sense.

That kind of clarity makes the athlete easier to evaluate.

Recruiting gets stronger when the message is not just, “I want to play for you.”

It is, “Here is why this could be a real fit.”

#CollegeRecruiting #RecruitingStrategy #StudentAthlete #SportsParents #RecruitNation

A recruiting email does not need to sound fancy.

It needs to sound intentional.

When an athlete can explain why a school makes sense, it shows preparation, maturity, and real interest.

That matters because coaches are not just evaluating talent. They are evaluating whether the athlete understands the opportunity and can communicate like someone ready for the next level.

A stronger message usually starts with a stronger reason.

Save this before your athlete sends another coach email.

#CollegeRecruiting #RecruitingTips #StudentAthlete #SportsParents #CoachCommunication

Most families think recruiting is about getting seen.

It is.

But that is only part of it.

Because once a coach starts watching, the evaluation goes way beyond talent.

They notice the eye roll.

They notice the slow jog back.

They notice the excuses.

They notice the teammate who picks someone up.

They notice the athlete who keeps competing when nothing is going right.

They notice who prepares.

They notice who owns mistakes.

They notice who is real.

Talent gets you noticed.

Consistency gets you recruited.

Build your floor.

Chase your ceiling.
items.transcript: So I've played at the professional level, I've coached at the division one level, I've evaluated thousands of high school athletes over the last 15 years, and while every sport is different, the traits college coaches trust are usually the same. Here are a few recruiting truths I've learned. Failure is inevitable, how you respond to it is a choice. Sports reward resilience more than talent gets credit for. Body language is visibly loud. You never know who is watching. Talent gets you noticed. Consistency gets you recruited. Nobody cares how good you were last week. Athletes who blame others rarely improve and coaches will see that. Confidence comes from preparation, not motivation. The game owes you nothing. Coaches trust competitors before they trust tools. Your teammates know if you are real. Coaches will ask your teammates about you. Competition has a way of exposing excuses. The best athletes are usually obsessed with improvement, not attention. Nobody remembers your excuses, they remember your actions. The athletes who separate themselves are not always the most talented. They are the ones who keep showing up every single day. They respond the right way when things go wrong. They compete when they are tired. They are prepared when nobody is watching. They take ownership instead of looking for someone else to blame. That is what college coaches are trying to find. Build your floor, chase your ceiling. Good luck.

items.caption:
College coaches can usually tell when the parent is driving the entire recruiting process.

Not always from one message.

But from the pattern.

Who asks the questions.
Who follows up.
Who talks on visits.
Who understands the school.
Who owns the opportunity.

Parents should absolutely support the process, but the athlete still has to take ownership.

That does not mean parents disappear.

It means parents help create structure while the athlete learns how to lead.

The parent can help organize the school list, track communication, ask better questions, and keep the process moving.

But the athlete should be the one learning about the program, sending the message, following up, and explaining why the school fits.

That is part of becoming recruitable.

Coaches are not just evaluating talent.

They are also watching maturity, communication, ownership, and how the athlete handles responsibility.

Save this if your family is trying to find the right parent-athlete balance in recruiting.
Share it with a sports parent who needs this reminder.

#CollegeRecruiting #StudentAthlete #SportsParents #RecruitingTips #RecruitNation

Most camp invites are NOT what parents think they are.

As a former Division 1 coach, scout, and recruiter, I can tell you this:

Just because your athlete got invited to a camp does NOT automatically mean they’re being heavily recruited.

A lot of these emails are mass blasts.

That’s why parents get frustrated…

They spend thousands on camps, hotels, flights, and showcases hoping for traction… and nothing happens.

Before committing to ANY camp, ask these 2 questions:

1️⃣ “Are you recruiting my position in my grad class?”
2️⃣ “Can you give feedback on my video?”

That second question is huge.

Because coaches who are genuinely interested usually engage.
Coaches who aren’t… usually don’t reply.

That alone can save families a ton of wasted time and money.

Then filter everything through:
✔️ Athletic fit
✔️ Academic fit
✔️ Geographic fit
✔️ Financial fit
✔️ Actual recruiting need

The families that win in recruiting usually aren’t attending the MOST events…

They’re attending the RIGHT ones.
items.transcript: I know decoding camp invites from schools and coaches could be a lot. You get a million of them and it's hard to filter through and hard to know what's legit, what's not. And if you're going to invest in a camp and go and travel to a camp, you want to make sure that it's worth it and that there's real opportunity there. And I was just talking to a parent, and hopefully this helps you, and we were filtering down their camps to make sure that they're going to the right ones. And a couple things to think about. One, reply back to the coach asking if they're in need of your position in your grad class, because not every school's in need of every position each year. So you want to make sure that they're in need of your position in your grad class. If you have a highlight video, include that in your email, preferably a YouTube link so they can click on it and it's easy for them to watch and it needs to be short as well. They don't want long highlight videos. And ask for feedback. If you don't get a reply, there's your answer right there, right? You don't need to go to the camp. It was probably a blast email. Other things to think about. Athletic standards. What are the athletic standards of that school? Does your athlete or do you come close to those athletic standards or projecting out to be? And it depends on your grade, right? If you're older, you need to be a little bit closer. If you're younger, not as much, but projecting out. And then academic standards, of course, can get into the school. You're close to the academic standards. Geographically, I would recommend staying somewhat local so it's easy for the college coaches to come watch you at your high school games or travel from there. Good luck.

items.caption:
Most families are chasing logos…
Smart families are chasing FIT.

Because the “best” school for your athlete isn’t just about athletics. It’s about:

→ Academics
→ Campus environment
→ Opportunity
→ Development
→ Network
→ Student experience
→ And finding a place where they can actually thrive for 4 years.

As a former Division 1 coach, scout, and recruiter, I can tell you this:

Too many athletes waste years targeting schools that were never the right fit in the first place.

The families that win in recruiting usually aren’t the ones hoping to get discovered…

They’re the ones with a real strategy.

Georgia has some unbelievable universities that can open doors athletically AND academically if you know how to position yourself the right way.

Which school would your athlete choose? 👇

Comment the school name below and I’ll tell you:
• What level athlete typically gets recruited there
• What coaches look for
• And how realistic that fit is right now

Save this for later because choosing the right fit can change your athlete’s entire future.

A lot of families are doing all the things…

Tournaments.
Showcases.
Camps.
Emails.
Highlight videos.

But there’s still no traction.

Here’s why:

College coaches usually do not walk into events blindly hoping to discover players.

They already have names they want to see.

So if your athlete is not being positioned before the event, there’s a good chance they are just hoping to get noticed.

And hope is not a recruiting strategy.

The goal is to target the right schools, understand who actually needs your athlete’s position, and get in front of those coaches before they show up.

That’s how you flip the script.

You stop waiting to be recruited.

You start recruiting the schools.

If you want a better plan to help your athlete get seen by the right college coaches, comment NATION and I’ll send you the playbook.
items.transcript: As a parent, I know it can be super frustrating helping your athlete go through this recruiting process, and there's nothing really happening. There's no traction, and you're doing all the things, and I know there's a million different opinions out there, and you're doing all the tournaments, showcases, and camps. But again, there's nothing happening. It's really out of like a hope, right? Hopefully somebody will discover them. Hopefully a school will discover my athlete. And you know that hope is not a good strategy going through this, really through anything. It's where you're proactive, right? You are going after. You're flipping the script. You are recruiting the schools, and you're going after them because these college coaches, as a former D1 coach, scout, and recruiter, these coaches have a list of athletes that are going to see at their tournaments or camps or showcases, and you got to get on that list. So you got to think about, well, how do I get on that list? Sitting back, just going to a random tournament, that's not how you get on a list, right? You be proactive in this recruiting process. You target the schools that are going to be a good fit for you, not only athletically, but academically, geographically, and you target those schools. If you're looking for a better plan to do that, you want something to follow that's proven, that's helped hundreds and hundreds of athletes, comment Nation down below, and I'll send it to you.

items.caption:
This is one of the biggest lies in recruiting:

“If you’re good enough, they’ll find you.”

For the top 1%? Yeah, for sure.

For everyone else, that mindset gets good players missed.

College coaches cannot recruit players they do not know about.

And today, part of the recruiting process is learning how to market yourself the right way.

That does not mean acting bigger than you are.

It does not mean posting fake hype.

It does not mean pretending to be perfect.

It means making it easy for the right coaches to evaluate you.

Your profile should clearly show:

Position
Grad year
Location
Top metrics
Grades
Video
Contact information

Then start posting your best qualities as an athlete.

Game video is great.

But practice/training video can work too.

Start with just showing your best qualities as a player - your best skill, and go from there.

College coaches want to see athletes.

They want to discover players.

They expect you to help them find you.

So stop worrying about what everyone else thinks.

Your goal is not to impress random people.

Your goal is to get in front of the right college coaches.

Stop waiting to be discovered.

Start positioning yourself.
items.transcript: If you're an athlete struggling to market yourself to college, coaches, whether via social media or highlight videos, one, I get it, but I want to give you a different perspective on this. As a former D1 coach, scout, and recruiter. And listen, I was there in high school struggling with this. I kind of just wanted to put my head down and work and train. But when I had this other perspective put into my head, and I wasn't an elite of elite athletes, you know that old saying of like, hey, if you're good enough, they'll find you. Man, that's only true for the top 1% of athletes. If you're a good athlete, but nobody knows about you, they're not just going to randomly find you. They will find the draft pick athletes for sure. But college coaches are expecting you to market. They want you to market yourself. That's how they find athletes. So I don't want you to going in of like, hey, what will other people think about me? What will coaches think about me? As a college scout recruiter, we want to see videos. We want to discover you. We want you to market yourself. So think about this. When you are marketing, focus on it doesn't all have to be game video. Game video can be good, but it can be just your best skills and training. Show off your best qualities as a player. That's what you want to get. Just start there. Start with your best quality as an athlete and post that and start following other coaches, other schools out there to help you because they get notified when you follow them, they get notified. And if you have a right profile, which is all of your top metrics, stats, and grades, where you're from, your position, your grad year on your profile, that is a good starting point to start marketing yourself. Good luck.

items.caption:
When coaches do not reply, families usually assume the worst.

But silence can mean a lot of things.

The school may not fit.

The message may be too generic.

The coach may need more information.

The timing may be wrong.

The follow-up may be missing.

Recruit Nation helps families understand what to adjust instead of guessing.
Inside, you get your athlete’s personal playbook for their sport and year, coach message templates, coaching support, monthly calls, and a private community for questions.

Comment PLAN if you want your athlete’s personal playbook plus coaching support inside Recruit Nation.

#CollegeRecruiting #CoachCommunication #SportsParents #StudentAthlete #RecruitNation
